class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Calls are growing for Republican Ben Carson to quit the 2016 race after he said a Muslim should not be president.</b></p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Muslim-American groups, among others, have said these views make him unqualified to run himself.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Mr Carson, a Christian, made the comments on Sunday, adding that Islam was inconsistent with the Constitution.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Another Republican presidential candidate, Bobby Jindal, said on Monday a Muslim president should swear on a Bible to uphold the Constitution.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">He said a Muslim Republican who fought to protect religious liberty, respected the Judeo-Christian heritage of the US and was committed to destroying Islamic State and radical Islam, and condemned cultures that treated women as second class citizens would get his vote.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">But they must "place their hand on the Bible and swear to uphold the Constitution", he added.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">The Republican candidates for president are being asked about their views on Islam since Donald Trump failed last week to correct a supporter who said President Barack Obama was a Muslim.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Mr Trump, the Republican frontrunner, has since said he would have no problem appointing a Muslim to his cabinet.</p></div></div></div></div><div data-block="image" class="ssrcss-l7emlr-ComponentWrapper e1aon0op1"><div class="ssrcss-oyhass-Spacer e1aon0op0"><div data-testid="image" editor</span></h2></div></div></div><div data-block="text" class="ssrcss-vxwoax-ComponentWrapper e1aon0op1"><div class="ssrcss-oyhass-Spacer e1aon0op0"><div data-testid="rich-text"><div class="ssrcss-nqezkk-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">I don't know whether Dr Carson's words will bolster his position among the base after a somewhat lacklustre performance in the Republican debate last week at the Ronald Reagan Library - but what about the feelings of the three million Muslims who live in America? </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">How is it going to feel to have a serious political figure, someone who aspires to lead this nation, essentially saying being Muslim is un-American? </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">These people who day in, day out serve their communities, support their families, enrich the lives of fellow citizens and make America the successful melting pot that it is.</p></div></div></div></div><div data-block="image" class="ssrcss-l7emlr-ComponentWrapper e1aon0op1"><div class="ssrcss-oyhass-Spacer e1aon0op0"><div data-testid="image" class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Ben Carson: "I would not advocate that we put a Muslim in charge of this nation"</p></div></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div data-block="text" class="ssrcss-vxwoax-ComponentWrapper e1aon0op1"><div class="ssrcss-oyhass-Spacer e1aon0op0"><div data-testid="rich-text"><div class="ssrcss-nqezkk-RichTextContainer e5tfeyi1"><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Democrats immediately denounced his remarks and Muslim groups called on Mr Carson, a neurosurgeon who is riding high in the polls among Republican voters, to quit the race.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">"To me this really means he is not qualified to be president of the United States," said Ibrahim Hooper, spokesman for the Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR). </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">He noted that the US Constitution specifically does not require the president to have a certain religion. </p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Farhana Khera of Muslim Advocates said the "religious bigotry" was heard when JFK was hoping to be the first Catholic president.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">And Haroon Moghul, a leading commentator on Islam, told CNN: "I think Carson's comments mean he should get out of the race".</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">One of the latest polls, run by CNN/ORC, shows Mr Carson has slipped into third place in the Republican race, trailing former Hewlett-Packard chief Carly Fiorina and Mr Trump, who has consistently remained ahead of the pack.</p><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10">Senior Republicans had said after the 2012 election that if the party did not become more inclusive, it would continue to push away young voters.</p></div></div></div></div><div data-block="image" class="ssrcss-l7emlr-ComponentWrapper e1aon0op1"><div class="ssrcss-oyhass-Spacer Now let's move on," the former secretary of state said on <a href="https://twitter.com/HillaryClinton/status/645989733055758336" class="ssrcss-1e0jzsh-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0">Twitter<span class="visually-hidden ssrcss-i2z2ig-VisuallyHidden e16en2lz0">, <!-- -->external</span></a>.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Ted Cruz</b>: "The Constitution specifies there shall be no religious test for public office and I am a constitutionalist," the Texas senator told an Iowa public television station.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Lindsey Graham</b>: "I think Dr Carson needs to apologise," the South Carolina senator told Fox News.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Mike Huckabee</b>: "I don't think we ought to just disqualify from somebody because of his or her faith," the former Arkansas governor told Fox Business Network.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">John Kasich</b>: "The most important thing about being president is you have leadership skills, you know what you're doing, and you can help fix this country and raise this country," the Ohio governor said on <a href="http://www.nbcnews.com/meet-the-press/meet-press-transcript-september-20-2015-n430581" class="ssrcss-1e0jzsh-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0">NBC's Meet the Press<span class="visually-hidden ssrcss-i2z2ig-VisuallyHidden e16en2lz0">, <!-- -->external</span></a>.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Rand Paul</b>: "I try to see that as a separate thing, someone's religion. I just think it's hard for us. We were attacked by people who were all Muslim," the Kentucky senator told <a href="http://www.cbsnews.com/news/face-the-nation-transcripts-september-20-clinton-and-paul/" class="ssrcss-1e0jzsh-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0">CBS News<span class="visually-hidden ssrcss-i2z2ig-VisuallyHidden e16en2lz0">, <!-- -->external</span></a>.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Bernie Sanders</b>: "People should be elected to office based on their ideas, not their religion or the colour of their skin," the Vermont senator said in a statement.</p></li><li><p class="ssrcss-1q0x1qg-Paragraph e1jhz7w10"><b class="ssrcss-1xjjfut-BoldText e5tfeyi3">Donald Trump</b>: "Some people have said it already happened, frankly," the real estate mogul said on <a href="http://www.nbcnews.com/meet-the-press/meet-press-transcript-september-20-2015-n430581" class="ssrcss-1e0jzsh-InlineLink e1kn3p7n0">NBC's Meet the gold_text: Calls are growing for Republican Ben Carson to quit the 2016 race after he said a Muslim should not be president. Muslim-American groups, among others, have said these views make him unqualified to run himself. Mr Carson, a Christian, made the comments on Sunday, adding that Islam was inconsistent with the Constitution. Another Republican presidential candidate, Bobby Jindal, said on Monday a Muslim president should swear on a Bible to uphold the Constitution. He said a Muslim Republican who fought to protect religious liberty, respected the Judeo-Christian heritage of the US and was committed to destroying Islamic State and radical Islam, and condemned cultures that treated women as second class citizens would get his vote. But they must "place their hand on the Bible and swear to uphold the Constitution", he added. The Republican candidates for president are being asked about their views on Islam since Donald Trump failed last week to correct a supporter who said President Barack Obama was a Muslim. Mr Trump, the Republican frontrunner, has since said he would have no problem appointing a Muslim to his cabinet. But in an interview with NBC's Meet the Press, Mr Carson said the president's faith matters if it differs with the values of America. "I would not advocate that we put a Muslim in charge of this nation," he said. He elaborated later when he told The Hill, external: "Muslims feel that their religion is very much a part of your public life and what you do as a public official, and that's inconsistent with our principles and our Constitution." Analysis - Jon Sopel, BBC North America editor I don't know whether Dr Carson's words will bolster his position among the base after a somewhat lacklustre performance in the Republican debate last week at the Ronald Reagan Library - but what about the feelings of the three million Muslims who live in America? How is it going to feel to have a serious political figure, someone who aspires to lead this nation, essentially saying being Muslim is un-American? These people who day in, day out serve their communities, support their families, enrich the lives of fellow citizens and make America the successful melting pot that it is. Democrats immediately denounced his remarks and Muslim groups called on Mr Carson, a neurosurgeon who is riding high in the polls among Republican voters, to quit the race. "To me this really means he is not qualified to be president of the United States," said Ibrahim Hooper, spokesman for the Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR). He noted that the US Constitution specifically does not require the president to have a certain religion. Farhana Khera of Muslim Advocates said the "religious bigotry" was heard when JFK was hoping to be the first Catholic president. And Haroon Moghul, a leading commentator on Islam, told CNN: "I think Carson's comments mean he should get out of the race". One of the latest polls, run by CNN/ORC, shows Mr Carson has slipped into third place in the Republican race, trailing former Hewlett-Packard chief Carly Fiorina and Mr Trump, who has consistently remained ahead of the pack. Senior Republicans had said after the 2012 election that if the party did not become more inclusive, it would continue to push away young voters. Should a Muslim be US president? YES Hillary Clinton: "In a word: Yes. Now let's move on," the former secretary of state said on Twitter, external. Ted Cruz: "The Constitution specifies there shall be no religious test for public office and I am a constitutionalist," the Texas senator told an Iowa public television station. Lindsey Graham: "I think Dr Carson needs to apologise," the South Carolina senator told Fox News. Mike Huckabee: "I don't think we ought to just disqualify from somebody because of his or her faith," the former Arkansas governor told Fox Business Network. John Kasich: "The most important thing about being president is you have leadership skills, you know what you're doing, and you can help fix this country and raise this country," the Ohio governor said on NBC's Meet the Press, external. Rand Paul: "I try to see that as a separate thing, someone's religion. I just think it's hard for us. We were attacked by people who were all Muslim," the Kentucky senator told CBS News, external. Bernie Sanders: "People should be elected to office based on their ideas, not their religion or the colour of their skin," the Vermont senator said in a statement. Donald Trump: "Some people have said it already happened, frankly," the real estate mogul said on NBC's Meet the Press, external. MAYBE Bobby Jindal: "If you can find me a Muslim candidate... who will place their hand on the Bible and swear to uphold the Constitution, then yes, I will be happy to consider voting for him or her," the Louisiana governor said in a statement. NO Ben Carson: "I would not advocate that we put a Muslim in charge of this nation. I absolutely would not agree with that," the retired neurosurgeon said.

ciXMDU"><div data-testid="hero-image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" Iranian flag, and a silhouette of Donald Trump on the right" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="712"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">BBC</span></div></div></figure></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In the middle of an Easter lunch at the White House, President Donald Trump went off script to address speculation about JD Vance's role in securing a deal to end the war in Iran.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"If it doesn't happen, I'm blaming JD Vance," Trump joked, drawing laughter at last week's East Room event attended by senior administration officials including the vice-president, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth. And "if it does happen," Trump added, "I'm taking full credit."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The remarks perfectly captured Vance's predicament as he leads a US delegation holding talks with Iran in Pakistan. It is the most challenging assignment of Vance's vice-presidency so far - one with a limited upside and plenty to lose if negotiations fail.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Vance's diplomatic mission to Islamabad is a political minefield. To make progress in reaching a permanent agreement to end the war, he will have to satisfy several stakeholders with competing interests, and who all distrust each other after a six-week military campaign that has engulfed the Middle East and roiled the global economy.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">US allies are watching Vance closely to see how he'll perform, one European official said. Vance "needs to step into the room and deliver something," added the official, who spoke on the condition of anonymity. "Otherwise he will be diminished."</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/f56c/live/f1a283b0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Inc via Getty Images President Donald Trump mimics firing a gun during a news conference in the White House briefing room " class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="474"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Inc via Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">The US president says he's delivering peace through strength</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Any deal must win the support first and foremost of Trump, who has vacillated between calling for peace and threatening to destroy Iran's civilisation. It will also need the backing of a weakened but still-standing regime in Tehran that has tightened control over the Strait of Hormuz, and an ally in Israel that is wary of a region-wide ceasefire. US allies in Europe that oppose the war and have been reluctant to come to America's aid in reopening the strait will also have to be convinced.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">As if that's not enough, Vance will also face pressure to somehow satisfy Trump's Make America Great Again (MAGA) base. Many are opposed to interventions abroad so will be watching his trip closely for clues about how he might handle foreign policy if he runs for president in 2028. </p></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 bKjgsR"><div class="sc-f361b62d-0 bNNbdd"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-0 djNPjD"><div data-testid="dotcom-sid_1" id="dotcom-sid_1" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_1" id="dotcom-mid_1" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">A former Marine who served in Iraq, Vance has been a vocal opponent of US entanglements in the past, and reportedly expressed deep scepticism about launching strikes on Iran in private meetings with Trump, according to a New York Times report.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"Vance has signalled a desire for restraint in American foreign policy. That's pretty hard to square with the American war against Iran," said Jeff Rathke, the president of the American-German Institute, a Washington-based think tank.</p><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp">Satisfying a demanding boss</h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In Islamabad the question for Vance is: can he make everyone happy? And what does success in these negotiations look like - a fully-formed peace deal, or just productive initial talks that don't scuttle the temporary ceasefire?</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">A White House official told the BBC that Trump had tasked the vice-president to lead the negotiations. And spokeswoman Anna Kelly confirmed that Vance had already been collaborating with special envoy Steve Witkoff and the president's son-in-law Jared Kushner, both of whom will be with him in Pakistan. </p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Vance tamped down expectations before leaving Washington on Friday morning.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"If the Iranians are willing to negotiate in good faith, we are certainly willing to extend an open hand," Vance told reporters. He also warned Iran not to "play us," and said Trump gave the US negotiating team "some pretty clear guidelines".</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">But even if he laid out clear goalposts ahead of time, the president has a propensity to change his mind. In the talks with Iran, Vance and the rest of the US team will face the added challenge of representing a mercurial boss in Trump who has given a range of rationales for the war since launching the conflict in late February.</p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_2" id="dotcom-mid_2" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">On Friday afternoon, when asked what he told Vance before he left for Islamabad, Trump told reporters: "I wish him luck. He's got a big thing." The president said he was sending a "good team" and "we'll see how it all turns out."</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/bef3/live/e8c958e0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="AFP via Getty Images Iranians hold national flags beneath a large billboard reading "The Strait of Hormuz remains closed" as people gather in Tehran's Revolution Square" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="474"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">AFP via Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Control of the Strait of Hormuz means the weakened Iranian regime still has leverage</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Trump's volatile negotiating style was on full display this week, in the whirlwind period leading up to the ceasefire deal reached on Tuesday. In a single 36-hour span, Trump gave Iran one day to strike a deal, warned in social media posts on Truth Social that "a whole civilisation will die" if Iran refused to cooperate, and then finally announced a ceasefire with less than two hours left before his deadline for escalating the war.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The tense hours on Tuesday were as nervewracking as any moments in Trump's second term, said a second senior European diplomat.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"I went home from work, put on the news, and started refreshing Truth Social," said the official, who spoke on the condition of anonymity. Serving as Trump's vice-president can't be easy under normal conditions, the diplomat added, but must be especially difficult for Vance right now given his misgivings about foreign wars.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"Vance has tried to distance himself from the Iran campaign," the source said. "This war is absolutely not in his playbook."</p><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp">Ambassador for 'America First'</h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Representing Trump in high-stakes negotiations may be a challenge, but Vance has been working toward this moment since taking office. He has earned Trump's trust and been given a seat at the table in high-profile meetings and events with foreign policy leaders in the Oval Office and across Europe and Asia.</p></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 bKjgsR"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-0 djNPjD"><div data-testid="dotcom-sid_2" id="dotcom-sid_2" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_3" id="dotcom-mid_3" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Vance made waves with a speech at the Munich Security Conference last year, where he issued a blistering critique of Europe's handling of immigration and free speech. Soon after, he accused Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky of not being grateful enough for US aid during an explosive Oval Office meeting that descended into a shouting match. Just this week, the vice-president travelled to Hungary to make an unprecedented re-election push for the current prime minister and close Trump ally Viktor Orbán.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">At each step, Vance has positioned himself as a loyal lieutenant to Trump who's willing to crisscross the globe serving as a sharp-elbowed ambassador of "America First".</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The vice-president is the "perfect surrogate" in these high-stakes negotiations because of "his firm understanding of President Trump's goals", said Mark Bednar, who worked on the Trump-Vance transition team. "To also have the trust of the president the way he does, that is a really great combination."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">But Vance reportedly hasn't always agreed with the president. And Trump's own foreign policy has sometimes put Vance in the awkward position of publicly backing the type of interventions abroad that he has argued against in the past - Iran being the prime example.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/7ac7/live/fa15c0c0-34c4-11f1-8606-05fe34b06e1b.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Reuters A man wears a shirt supporting U.S. President Donald Trump and Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="474"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Reuters</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Trump's supporters are devoted - but not all support the war in Iran</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">As a US senator Vance published a Wall Street Journal op-ed in 2023 saying he backed Trump because he didn't start foreign wars. On the eve of the Iran war, Vance told the Washington Post that Trump wouldn't let the US get dragged into another so-called forever war in the Middle East.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">During the current conflict, he has publicly echoed Trump's argument that war was needed to prevent Iran from developing nuclear weapons. But Vance has not been as vocal in backing the war effort as other administration officials, in particular Defence Secretary Hegseth. Vance's rhetoric has also sometimes diverged from Trump's, as happened this week when Vance called the temporary ceasefire a "fragile truce".</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Despite this, Trump tapped Vance to lead the US delegation to Islamabad. The choice raised questions of whether Trump purposely handed Vance a no-win assignment. The vice-president's office declined to comment for this story, but a US official who asked not to be named said Vance was chosen to send Iran a signal that the Trump administration was serious about reaching a deal.</p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_4" id="dotcom-mid_4" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp">No easy fix</h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">US allies in the region welcomed Vance's inclusion on the team as a sign the administration wants a durable peace to end the war. "It shows that America is seriously coming to the table," said Assaf Orion, a retired Israeli brigadier general.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Vance will have to tune out the chatter back in Washington to deliver in negotiations with Iran.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The Oval Office confrontation with Zelensky and trips to Munich and Budapest all showcased Vance's ability to play the role of attack dog.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"If he feels the need to be argumentative, he's not going to shy away from that. If he feels that something is not going right for the United States, he will not shy away from that either," said Mark Bednar, the Republican strategist.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">But in Pakistan he's playing a different diplomatic role, which will require navigating tense relations between adversaries in a volatile region. Vance and his team will also have to address a complex set of problems - including reopening the Strait of Hormuz and ending Iran's nuclear programme - that are highly technical and have no easy fix.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/33f3/live/f5a56e50-34c4-11f1-9d5c-8ba507d7dbde.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Getty Images Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu (L) speaks alongside U.S. President Donald Trump, Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio during a meeting in the Oval Office of the White House." class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="474"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Vance has had a prominent place for Oval Office encounters with world leaders</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Vance was not involved at a detailed level in the indirect talks between the US and Iran before the ceasefire. Negotiations in recent weeks were led by Witkoff and Kushner, according to a US official. The pair also spearheaded other peace talks during Trump's second term. The success of the talks will rely in part on who handles technical aspects of Iran's nuclear programme, Orion said.</p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_5" id="dotcom-mid_5" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"Kushner and Witkoff did Ukraine, Iran and Gaza. I think it's very challenging to be a detailed expert in all three issues at the same time," Orion said. In Islamabad, "the question is who else, who is on the bench?"</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Still Vance will command the most attention, regardless of whoever else is in the room. </p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The vice-president won't be able to rely on a deep well of personal relationships with foreign leaders built up over years. It can be easy to forget Vance is still just 41 years old, and only recently launched himself onto the national political stage when he ran for the US Senate. For all of his polish and shrewd instincts, he is still a relative newcomer to the world of international relations.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Nevertheless, Trump threw him into the deep end this week, and now Vance is under pressure to deliver a victory for the president while not damaging his own political future.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"He's not negotiating JD Vance's agreement with the Iranians. He's there in Islamabad to try to get the best deal the president can agree to," said Rathke. "But that has some risks for the vice-president, in case Trump agrees to something and then later loses his enthusiasm for it. Trump may try to blame the negotiator."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"><i id="additional-reporting-by-grace-eliza-goodwin" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">Additional reporting by Grace Eliza Goodwin</i></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"></p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_6" id="dotcom-mid_6" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 data-component="tag-list-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 hoFfRa"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-6bf80075-0 lllwyY"><div href="/news/topics/cjnwl8q4ggwt" data-testid="internal-link" class="sc-57521d02-0 jsVxCb">Iran</a></div><div data-testid="anchor-inner-wrapper"><a href="/news/topics/cp7r8vgl2lgt" gold_text: In the middle of an Easter lunch at the White House, President Donald Trump went off script to address speculation about JD Vance's role in securing a deal to end the war in Iran. "If it doesn't happen, I'm blaming JD Vance," Trump joked, drawing laughter at last week's East Room event attended by senior administration officials including the vice-president, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, and Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth. And "if it does happen," Trump added, "I'm taking full credit." The remarks perfectly captured Vance's predicament as he leads a US delegation holding talks with Iran in Pakistan. It is the most challenging assignment of Vance's vice-presidency so far - one with a limited upside and plenty to lose if negotiations fail. Vance's diplomatic mission to Islamabad is a political minefield. To make progress in reaching a permanent agreement to end the war, he will have to satisfy several stakeholders with competing interests, and who all distrust each other after a six-week military campaign that has engulfed the Middle East and roiled the global economy. US allies are watching Vance closely to see how he'll perform, one European official said. Vance "needs to step into the room and deliver something," added the official, who spoke on the condition of anonymity. "Otherwise he will be diminished." Any deal must win the support first and foremost of Trump, who has vacillated between calling for peace and threatening to destroy Iran's civilisation. It will also need the backing of a weakened but still-standing regime in Tehran that has tightened control over the Strait of Hormuz, and an ally in Israel that is wary of a region-wide ceasefire. US allies in Europe that oppose the war and have been reluctant to come to America's aid in reopening the strait will also have to be convinced. As if that's not enough, Vance will also face pressure to somehow satisfy Trump's Make America Great Again (MAGA) base. Many are opposed to interventions abroad so will be watching his trip closely for clues about how he might handle foreign policy if he runs for president in 2028. A former Marine who served in Iraq, Vance has been a vocal opponent of US entanglements in the past, and reportedly expressed deep scepticism about launching strikes on Iran in private meetings with Trump, according to a New York Times report. "Vance has signalled a desire for restraint in American foreign policy. That's pretty hard to square with the American war against Iran," said Jeff Rathke, the president of the American-German Institute, a Washington-based think tank. Satisfying a demanding boss In Islamabad the question for Vance is: can he make everyone happy? And what does success in these negotiations look like - a fully-formed peace deal, or just productive initial talks that don't scuttle the temporary ceasefire? A White House official told the BBC that Trump had tasked the vice-president to lead the negotiations. And spokeswoman Anna Kelly confirmed that Vance had already been collaborating with special envoy Steve Witkoff and the president's son-in-law Jared Kushner, both of whom will be with him in Pakistan. Vance tamped down expectations before leaving Washington on Friday morning. "If the Iranians are willing to negotiate in good faith, we are certainly willing to extend an open hand," Vance told reporters. He also warned Iran not to "play us," and said Trump gave the US negotiating team "some pretty clear guidelines". But even if he laid out clear goalposts ahead of time, the president has a propensity to change his mind. In the talks with Iran, Vance and the rest of the US team will face the added challenge of representing a mercurial boss in Trump who has given a range of rationales for the war since launching the conflict in late February. On Friday afternoon, when asked what he told Vance before he left for Islamabad, Trump told reporters: "I wish him luck. He's got a big thing." The president said he was sending a "good team" and "we'll see how it all turns out." Trump's volatile negotiating style was on full display this week, in the whirlwind period leading up to the ceasefire deal reached on Tuesday. In a single 36-hour span, Trump gave Iran one day to strike a deal, warned in social media posts on Truth Social that "a whole civilisation will die" if Iran refused to cooperate, and then finally announced a ceasefire with less than two hours left before his deadline for escalating the war. The tense hours on Tuesday were as nervewracking as any moments in Trump's second term, said a second senior European diplomat. "I went home from work, put on the news, and started refreshing Truth Social," said the official, who spoke on the condition of anonymity. Serving as Trump's vice-president can't be easy under normal conditions, the diplomat added, but must be especially difficult for Vance right now given his misgivings about foreign wars. "Vance has tried to distance himself from the Iran campaign," the source said. "This war is absolutely not in his playbook." Ambassador for 'America First' Representing Trump in high-stakes negotiations may be a challenge, but Vance has been working toward this moment since taking office. He has earned Trump's trust and been given a seat at the table in high-profile meetings and events with foreign policy leaders in the Oval Office and across Europe and Asia. Vance made waves with a speech at the Munich Security Conference last year, where he issued a blistering critique of Europe's handling of immigration and free speech. Soon after, he accused Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky of not being grateful enough for US aid during an explosive Oval Office meeting that descended into a shouting match. Just this week, the vice-president travelled to Hungary to make an unprecedented re-election push for the current prime minister and close Trump ally Viktor Orbán. At each step, Vance has positioned himself as a loyal lieutenant to Trump who's willing to crisscross the globe serving as a sharp-elbowed ambassador of "America First". The vice-president is the "perfect surrogate" in these high-stakes negotiations because of "his firm understanding of President Trump's goals", said Mark Bednar, who worked on the Trump-Vance transition team. "To also have the trust of the president the way he does, that is a really great combination." But Vance reportedly hasn't always agreed with the president. And Trump's own foreign policy has sometimes put Vance in the awkward position of publicly backing the type of interventions abroad that he has argued against in the past - Iran being the prime example. As a US senator Vance published a Wall Street Journal op-ed in 2023 saying he backed Trump because he didn't start foreign wars. On the eve of the Iran war, Vance told the Washington Post that Trump wouldn't let the US get dragged into another so-called forever war in the Middle East. During the current conflict, he has publicly echoed Trump's argument that war was needed to prevent Iran from developing nuclear weapons. But Vance has not been as vocal in backing the war effort as other administration officials, in particular Defence Secretary Hegseth. Vance's rhetoric has also sometimes diverged from Trump's, as happened this week when Vance called the temporary ceasefire a "fragile truce". Despite this, Trump tapped Vance to lead the US delegation to Islamabad. The choice raised questions of whether Trump purposely handed Vance a no-win assignment. The vice-president's office declined to comment for this story, but a US official who asked not to be named said Vance was chosen to send Iran a signal that the Trump administration was serious about reaching a deal. No easy fix US allies in the region welcomed Vance's inclusion on the team as a sign the administration wants a durable peace to end the war. "It shows that America is seriously coming to the table," said Assaf Orion, a retired Israeli brigadier general. Vance will have to tune out the chatter back in Washington to deliver in negotiations with Iran. The Oval Office confrontation with Zelensky and trips to Munich and Budapest all showcased Vance's ability to play the role of attack dog. "If he feels the need to be argumentative, he's not going to shy away from that. If he feels that something is not going right for the United States, he will not shy away from that either," said Mark Bednar, the Republican strategist. But in Pakistan he's playing a different diplomatic role, which will require navigating tense relations between adversaries in a volatile region. Vance and his team will also have to address a complex set of problems - including reopening the Strait of Hormuz and ending Iran's nuclear programme - that are highly technical and have no easy fix. Vance was not involved at a detailed level in the indirect talks between the US and Iran before the ceasefire. Negotiations in recent weeks were led by Witkoff and Kushner, according to a US official. The pair also spearheaded other peace talks during Trump's second term. The success of the talks will rely in part on who handles technical aspects of Iran's nuclear programme, Orion said. "Kushner and Witkoff did Ukraine, Iran and Gaza. I think it's very challenging to be a detailed expert in all three issues at the same time," Orion said. In Islamabad, "the question is who else, who is on the bench?" Still Vance will command the most attention, regardless of whoever else is in the room. The vice-president won't be able to rely on a deep well of personal relationships with foreign leaders built up over years. It can be easy to forget Vance is still just 41 years old, and only recently launched himself onto the national political stage when he ran for the US Senate. For all of his polish and shrewd instincts, he is still a relative newcomer to the world of international relations. Nevertheless, Trump threw him into the deep end this week, and now Vance is under pressure to deliver a victory for the president while not damaging his own political future. "He's not negotiating JD Vance's agreement with the Iranians. He's there in Islamabad to try to get the best deal the president can agree to," said Rathke. "But that has some risks for the vice-president, in case Trump agrees to something and then later loses his enthusiasm for it. Trump may try to blame the negotiator."

class="sc-cd6075cf-0 laOREU"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 ciXMDU"><div data-testid="hero-image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/abda/live/91be1460-23a7-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/abda/live/91be1460-23a7-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/abda/live/91be1460-23a7-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp" loading="eager" alt="Alice & Bob A technician works on part of the Alice and Bob quantum computer. It looks like a large chandelier." class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="400"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Alice & Bob</span></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">A technician works on the cryostat that cools the Alice & Bob quantum computer</figcaption></div></figure></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In a lab on the western edge of Paris, where the River Seine flows wide and trams slide past glass-fronted buildings and blossoming cherry trees, a technician called Rémi makes some adjustments with a spanner.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The machine, a cascade of gold and silver-coloured cylinders descending through a cloud of wires, is a cryostat, a device that cools so much it slows activity even at the molecular level.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Minus 273 degrees Celsius in the cylinder at the bottom. A temperature at which even the tiniest particles are still. Isolation from the outside world is complete.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In this cylinder is placed a small case, again of gold and silver colour, in which is inserted a chip.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">This chip is another, tinier box inside of which takes place the phenomenon discovered by Albert Einstein and other physicists that seems to defy the mechanics of the world we live in: the quantum leap, when particles change energy levels in ways that are predictable, reproducible and apparently impossible.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Around us there are several vertical cylinders like so many different-sized water heaters. They all contain cryostats like the one Rémi is tinkering with. Oh, and these machines have another name. They are quantum computers.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Alice & Bob Théau Peronnin holds one of his company's computer chips and stands next to co-founder Raphaël Lescanne." class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="400"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Alice & Bob</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Théau Peronnin (left) and Raphaël Lescanne, the co-founders of Alice & Bob</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">This is the French quantum computer company Alice & Bob. In the next few months it will also open a much larger facility north of Paris, costing $50m (£37m), with a test and run facility to try out bigger and bigger machines, and a clean room where it will make its own chips.</p></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 bKjgsR"><div class="sc-f361b62d-0 bNNbdd"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-0 djNPjD"><div data-testid="dotcom-sid_1" id="dotcom-sid_1" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_1" id="dotcom-mid_1" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Alice & Bob may sound like an ice-cream company, but the 200-strong team of 20 and 30-somethings you see buzzing around this hive of a start-up do some very serious science, and will soon, says co-founder and CEO Théau Peronnin, be doing serious business too.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"Physicists used to doubt it was possible to leverage the weird behaviour of particles in the quantum. They don't anymore.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Now we know they work, and in a few years we will have reliable quantum computers that we can hook up to High Performance Computers (HPCs) in data centres to exponentially increase their computing power," says Peronnin.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"It's not about being faster. It's about being so dramatically faster that you change what is feasible. We will be able to solve problems that are absolutely intractable with classical computers," he says.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">For example? "It will make medicine an exact science." He is only half joking. "At the moment, the development of new medicines is largely a question of trial and error." </p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">With quantum computers, it will be possible to run massive computes to see how different molecules react with each other to find out what works and with what side-effects. </p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The prize, then, for the company that is first to build a reliable quantum computer at scale is potentially enormous. It will be "winner-takes-all", as it was for classical computers and IBM, predicts Peronnin. And, he reckons, there is a fighting chance that his or another French company will be that winner.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The biggest obstacle in quantum computing is fragility which leads to errors.</p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_2" id="dotcom-mid_2" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Conventional computers use electricity within silicon chips to make calculations.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In quantum computers that is done with qubits where, in each qubit, the quantum properties of an individual electron or photon are manipulated.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The big challenge for researchers is that qubits lose their delicate quantum states (decohere) due to noise from the everyday world. </p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Most approaches fight this with massive redundancy, explains Peronnin: thousands of physical qubits per "logical" one, using what he calls a "majority vote" to correct errors. The pay-off for that is daunting scale and cost.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Alice & Bob have taken a different approach.  Their "cat qubits" (<a target="_blank" href="https://www.sciencefocus.com/science/what-is-schrodingers-cat" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">named after Schrödinger's cat</a>) are designed to autonomously correct some errors.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"It's built-in by design," Peronnin says. "We cracked a way to compensate for losses continuously."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">This will, in theory, spectacularly reduce process complexity and cost compared to their redundancy-heavy rivals.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/259c/live/ee115ff0-23a8-11f1-b297-95b0a0a8331e.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Alice & Bob A blue computer chip from Alice & Bob" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="400"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Alice & Bob</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Alice & Bob has built a quantum computing chip called Boson</figcaption></figure></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 bKjgsR"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-0 djNPjD"><div data-testid="dotcom-sid_2" id="dotcom-sid_2" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_3" id="dotcom-mid_3" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Peronnin notes big players are shifting towards similar ideas: Google acquired Atlantic Quantum, while others are pursuing cousins of cat qubits. Alice & Bob now stands "shoulder to shoulder" with its US competitors, Peronnin says.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">They're one of France's "national champions" <a target="_blank" href="https://alice-bob.com/newsroom/alice-bob-funded-to-build-fault-tolerant-qunatum-computer-proqcima/" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">under the PROQCIMA program</a>, a government initiative to develop a useful quatum computer.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">And France has a lot more than Alice & Bob going on. In fact, its companies represent between them the full range of qubit types – all the routes physicists are hoping will lead to the first reliable and ultra-powerful quantum computer.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"If you look at the quantum computing world in France, we have six companies right now, and two being created," says Olivier Ezratty, an academic whose 1500-page compendium Understanding Quantum Technologies <a target="_blank" href="https://www.oezratty.net/wordpress/2025/understanding-quantum-technologies-2025/" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">is a popular free download</a> on the internet.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">He says there are four other "important" French quantum computing firms: Pasqal, Quandela, Quobly and C12.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Ezratty says they have one advantage in common. "Most of them are in a very favourable position in the cost of the machine and the energetic cost," he says.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 cMIbKV"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/240/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 240w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/320/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 320w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 480w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/640/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 640w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/800/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 800w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1024/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 1024w,https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/1536/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp 1536w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/news/480/cpsprodpb/2d86/live/db8401b0-23aa-11f1-9a4e-e13728524fa3.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Getty Images A bystander looks at the gold electronics of an IBM quantum computer" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="400"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Alice & Bob faces global competition including from IBM</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Elsewhere in Europe, the biggest player is Finland's IQM that announced in February that it would become the first listed European quantum company.</p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_4" id="dotcom-mid_4" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The UK has two significant players in Oxford Quantum Circuits (OQM) and quantum operating system creators Riverlane.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">IQM and Pasqal already have quantum computers that are contributing to high-performance computing (HPC) installations across Europe.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Some French firms already have quantum computers placed in companies such as industrial products firm, Air Liquide. This will soon be the case for Alice & Bob.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Their machines are not yet ready to fulfil the promise of quantum computing.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">But getting them into the wider world means that a community of specialists can be trained up, who will be ready to use the real thing when it happens.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"At the moment, the machine we have is no more powerful than your telephone," says Peronnin. "We're on the flat part of the exponential curve."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In the quantum race, France has a couple of strong points.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">It has some of the world's best physics teaching at schools such as École Polytechnique and École Normale Supérieure.</p></div></div></div></div><div class="ad-placeholder" style="display: contents; height: 293px;"><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 fAzFNb"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_5" id="dotcom-mid_5" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"In the past few years three Nobel Prizes have gone to French physicists alone!" Peronnin says. That is critical, he says, as it is a surprisingly-level playing field.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"At the end of the day, it's a math challenge. There is no unfair advantage from legacy technology like classical computing or something like that, so there is no reason to be shy."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The main challenge, says Peronnin, is putting the capital together. "But Europe is definitely not poor and this is a technological opportunity for Europe to reshuffle a bit the cards in terms of autonomous strategy and our ability to have economic leading players," he says.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">There is a strong feeling that while Europe missed the boat on so many tech revolutions of recent years, at least when it came to transitioning from the research to the industrial stage, things could be different this time.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"We have what it takes to win it. It's about believing in ourselves. We're always, as French, a bit mocking the overconfidence of Americans, but here we need to be a bit bullish. Otherwise, nothing is going to happen, and that would be really a shame because at the moment, we're in a far better position than anyone could have thought."</p><div class="sc-fa53feb6-0 sc-479a310b-0 eqHMcZ flPFpY"><span data-testid="links-title" class="sc-479a310b-2 fxCOCW">More A temperature at which even the tiniest particles are still. Isolation from the outside world is complete.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Minus 273 degrees Celsius in the cylinder at the bottom. A temperature at which even the tiniest particles are still. Isolation from the outside world is complete.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"In this cylinder is placed a small case, again of gold and silver colour, in which is inserted a chip.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"In this cylinder is placed a small case, again of gold and silver colour, in which is inserted a chip.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"This chip is another, tinier box inside of which takes place the phenomenon discovered by Albert Einstein and other physicists that seems to defy the mechanics of the world we live in: the quantum leap, when particles change energy levels in ways that are predictable, reproducible and apparently impossible.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"This chip is another, tinier box inside of which takes place the phenomenon discovered by Albert Einstein and other physicists that seems to defy the mechanics of the world we live in: the quantum leap, when particles change energy levels in ways that are predictable, reproducible and apparently impossible.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Around us there are several vertical cylinders like so many different-sized water heaters. They all contain cryostats like the one Rémi is tinkering with. Oh, and these machines have another name. They are quantum computers.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Around us there are several vertical cylinders like so many different-sized water heaters. They all contain cryostats like the one Rémi is tinkering with. Oh, and these machines have another name. They are quantum computers.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"advertisement","model":{"adType":"horizontal"},"incrementedType":"mid_1"},{"type":"image","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"caption","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Théau Peronnin (left) and Raphaël Lescanne, the co-founders of Alice \u0026 Bob","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Théau Peronnin (left) and Raphaël Lescanne, the co-founders of Alice \u0026 Bob","attributes":[]}}]}}]}}]}},{"type":"altText","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Théau Peronnin holds one of his company's computer chips and stands next to co-founder Raphaël Lescanne.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Théau Peronnin holds one of his company's computer chips and stands next to co-founder Raphaël Lescanne.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}}]}},{"type":"rawImage","model":{"width":3219,"height":1811,"locator":"f848/live/6d437a60-23a9-11f1-a79a-77e93010d956.jpg","originCode":"cpsprodpb","copyrightHolder":"Alice \u0026 Bob","suitableForSyndication":true}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"This is the French quantum computer company Alice \u0026 Bob. In the next few months it will also open a much larger facility north of Paris, costing $50m (£37m), with a test and run facility to try out bigger and bigger machines, and a clean room where it will make its own chips.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"This is the French quantum computer company Alice \u0026 Bob. In the next few months it will also open a much larger facility north of Paris, costing $50m (£37m), with a test and run facility to try out bigger and bigger machines, and a clean room where it will make its own chips.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Alice \u0026 Bob may sound like an ice-cream company, but the 200-strong team of 20 and 30-somethings you see buzzing around this hive of a start-up do some very serious science, and will soon, says co-founder and CEO Théau Peronnin, be doing serious business too.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Alice \u0026 Bob may sound like an ice-cream company, but the 200-strong team of 20 and 30-somethings you see buzzing around this hive of a start-up do some very serious science, and will soon, says co-founder and CEO Théau Peronnin, be doing serious business too.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"\"Physicists used to doubt it was possible to leverage the weird behaviour of particles in the quantum. They don't anymore.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"\"Physicists used to doubt it was possible to leverage the weird behaviour of particles in the quantum. They don't anymore.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Now we know they work, and in a few years we will have reliable quantum computers that we can hook up to High Performance Computers (HPCs) in data centres to exponentially increase their computing power,\" says Peronnin.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Now we know they work, and in a few years we will have reliable quantum computers that we can hook up to High Performance Computers (HPCs) in data centres to exponentially increase their computing power,\" says Peronnin.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"\"It's not about being faster. It's about being so dramatically faster that you change what is feasible. We will be able to solve problems that are absolutely intractable with classical computers,\" he says.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"\"It's not about being faster. It's about being so dramatically faster that you change what is feasible. We will be able to solve problems that are absolutely intractable with classical computers,\" he says.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"For example? \"It will make medicine an exact science.\" He is only half joking. \"At the moment, the development of new medicines is largely a question of trial and error.\" ","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"For example? \"It will make medicine an exact science.\" He is only half joking. \"At the moment, the development of new medicines is largely a question of trial and error.\" ","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"With quantum computers, it will be possible to run massive computes to see how different molecules react with each other to find out what works and with what side-effects. ","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"With quantum computers, it will be possible to run massive computes to see how different molecules react with each other to find out what works and with what side-effects. ","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"advertisement","model":{"adType":"horizontal"},"incrementedType":"mid_2"},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"The prize, then, for the company that is first to build a reliable quantum computer at scale is potentially enormous. It will be \"winner-takes-all\", as it was for classical computers and IBM, predicts Peronnin. And, he reckons, there is a fighting chance that his or another French company will be that winner.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"The prize, then, for the company that is first to build a reliable quantum computer at scale is potentially enormous. It will be \"winner-takes-all\", as it was for classical computers and IBM, predicts Peronnin. And, he reckons, there is a fighting chance that his or another French company will be that winner.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"The biggest obstacle in quantum computing is fragility which leads to errors.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"The biggest obstacle in quantum computing is fragility which leads to errors.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Conventional computers use electricity within silicon chips to make calculations.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Conventional computers use electricity within silicon chips to make calculations.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"In quantum computers that is done with qubits where, in each qubit, the quantum properties of an individual electron or photon are manipulated.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"In quantum computers that is done with qubits where, in each qubit, the quantum properties of an individual electron or photon are manipulated.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"The big challenge for researchers is that qubits lose their delicate quantum states (decohere) due to noise from the everyday world. ","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"The big challenge for researchers is that qubits lose their delicate quantum states (decohere) due to noise from the everyday world. ","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Most approaches fight this with massive redundancy, explains Peronnin: thousands of physical qubits per \"logical\" one, using what he calls a \"majority vote\" to correct errors.
