gold_text: In a lab on the western edge of Paris, where the River Seine flows wide and trams slide past glass-fronted buildings and blossoming cherry trees, a technician called Rémi makes some adjustments with a spanner. The machine, a cascade of gold and silver-coloured cylinders descending through a cloud of wires, is a cryostat, a device that cools so much it slows activity even at the molecular level. Minus 273 degrees Celsius in the cylinder at the bottom. A temperature at which even the tiniest particles are still. Isolation from the outside world is complete. In this cylinder is placed a small case, again of gold and silver colour, in which is inserted a chip. This chip is another, tinier box inside of which takes place the phenomenon discovered by Albert Einstein and other physicists that seems to defy the mechanics of the world we live in: the quantum leap, when particles change energy levels in ways that are predictable, reproducible and apparently impossible. Around us there are several vertical cylinders like so many different-sized water heaters. They all contain cryostats like the one Rémi is tinkering with. Oh, and these machines have another name. They are quantum computers. This is the French quantum computer company Alice & Bob. In the next few months it will also open a much larger facility north of Paris, costing $50m (£37m), with a test and run facility to try out bigger and bigger machines, and a clean room where it will make its own chips. Alice & Bob may sound like an ice-cream company, but the 200-strong team of 20 and 30-somethings you see buzzing around this hive of a start-up do some very serious science, and will soon, says co-founder and CEO Théau Peronnin, be doing serious business too. "Physicists used to doubt it was possible to leverage the weird behaviour of particles in the quantum. They don't anymore. Now we know they work, and in a few years we will have reliable quantum computers that we can hook up to High Performance Computers (HPCs) in data centres to exponentially increase their computing power," says Peronnin. "It's not about being faster. It's about being so dramatically faster that you change what is feasible. We will be able to solve problems that are absolutely intractable with classical computers," he says. For example? "It will make medicine an exact science." He is only half joking. "At the moment, the development of new medicines is largely a question of trial and error." With quantum computers, it will be possible to run massive computes to see how different molecules react with each other to find out what works and with what side-effects. The prize, then, for the company that is first to build a reliable quantum computer at scale is potentially enormous. It will be "winner-takes-all", as it was for classical computers and IBM, predicts Peronnin. And, he reckons, there is a fighting chance that his or another French company will be that winner. The biggest obstacle in quantum computing is fragility which leads to errors. Conventional computers use electricity within silicon chips to make calculations. In quantum computers that is done with qubits where, in each qubit, the quantum properties of an individual electron or photon are manipulated. The big challenge for researchers is that qubits lose their delicate quantum states (decohere) due to noise from the everyday world. Most approaches fight this with massive redundancy, explains Peronnin: thousands of physical qubits per "logical" one, using what he calls a "majority vote" to correct errors. The pay-off for that is daunting scale and cost. Alice & Bob have taken a different approach. Their "cat qubits" (named after Schrödinger's cat) are designed to autonomously correct some errors. "It's built-in by design," Peronnin says. "We cracked a way to compensate for losses continuously." This will, in theory, spectacularly reduce process complexity and cost compared to their redundancy-heavy rivals. Peronnin notes big players are shifting towards similar ideas: Google acquired Atlantic Quantum, while others are pursuing cousins of cat qubits. Alice & Bob now stands "shoulder to shoulder" with its US competitors, Peronnin says. They're one of France's "national champions" under the PROQCIMA program, a government initiative to develop a useful quatum computer. And France has a lot more than Alice & Bob going on. In fact, its companies represent between them the full range of qubit types – all the routes physicists are hoping will lead to the first reliable and ultra-powerful quantum computer. "If you look at the quantum computing world in France, we have six companies right now, and two being created," says Olivier Ezratty, an academic whose 1500-page compendium Understanding Quantum Technologies is a popular free download on the internet. He says there are four other "important" French quantum computing firms: Pasqal, Quandela, Quobly and C12. Ezratty says they have one advantage in common. "Most of them are in a very favourable position in the cost of the machine and the energetic cost," he says. Elsewhere in Europe, the biggest player is Finland's IQM that announced in February that it would become the first listed European quantum company. The UK has two significant players in Oxford Quantum Circuits (OQM) and quantum operating system creators Riverlane. IQM and Pasqal already have quantum computers that are contributing to high-performance computing (HPC) installations across Europe. Some French firms already have quantum computers placed in companies such as industrial products firm, Air Liquide. This will soon be the case for Alice & Bob. Their machines are not yet ready to fulfil the promise of quantum computing. But getting them into the wider world means that a community of specialists can be trained up, who will be ready to use the real thing when it happens. "At the moment, the machine we have is no more powerful than your telephone," says Peronnin. "We're on the flat part of the exponential curve." In the quantum race, France has a couple of strong points. It has some of the world's best physics teaching at schools such as École Polytechnique and École Normale Supérieure. "In the past few years three Nobel Prizes have gone to French physicists alone!" Peronnin says. That is critical, he says, as it is a surprisingly-level playing field. "At the end of the day, it's a math challenge. There is no unfair advantage from legacy technology like classical computing or something like that, so there is no reason to be shy." The main challenge, says Peronnin, is putting the capital together. "But Europe is definitely not poor and this is a technological opportunity for Europe to reshuffle a bit the cards in terms of autonomous strategy and our ability to have economic leading players," he says. There is a strong feeling that while Europe missed the boat on so many tech revolutions of recent years, at least when it came to transitioning from the research to the industrial stage, things could be different this time. "We have what it takes to win it. It's about believing in ourselves. We're always, as French, a bit mocking the overconfidence of Americans, but here we need to be a bit bullish. Otherwise, nothing is going to happen, and that would be really a shame because at the moment, we're in a far better position than anyone could have thought."

England's curved coastline (Credit: Getty Images)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="400"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">(Credit: Getty Images)</figcaption></div></figure></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"><b id="a-new-hiking-trail-will-soon-allow-travellers-to-walk-around-england's-entire-coast-–-but-a-strange-paradox-means-no-one-knows-exactly-how-long-it-is." class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">A new hiking trail will soon allow travellers to walk around England's entire coast – but a strange paradox means no one knows exactly how long it is.</b></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Last month, King Charles III inaugurated a new hiking path that will soon stretch around the entire coast of England. The project is roughly 80% complete, and when it fully opens later this year, the King Charles III England Coast Path will become the longest managed coastal walking path in the world.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The 2,689-mile-long (4,327km) ramble connects the granite cliffs of Cornwall with the rolling sand dunes of Northumberland and East Sussex's iconic white chalk cliffs, allowing travellers to explore England's extensive shoreline step by step.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">But while the length of the newly designed path is easily measurable, the coastline that it follows is not. England's coast is often measured as part of the UK's, but look up how long that is and you'll get wildly different answers from various reputable organisations. The CIA World Factbook lists the UK's coastline as 7,723 miles (12,429km), while The World Resources Institute measures it at 12,251 miles (19,716km) – a discrepancy of more than 4,500 miles (7,242km).</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"The thing is, no one really knows <i id="exactly" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">exactly</i> how long England's coastline is, or the United Kingdom's or most coastlines around the world, for that matter," said Victoria Braswell, a researcher and member of the Royal Geographical Society. "It's all in how you measure it."</p></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 bKjgsR"><div class="sc-f361b62d-0 bNNbdd"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-0 djNPjD"><div data-testid="dotcom-sid_1" id="dotcom-sid_1" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_1" id="dotcom-mid_1" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllrv.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Getty Images The King Charles III England Coast Path will soon allow travellers to walk the entire English coast (Credit: Getty Images)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA" width="712" height="400"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">The King Charles III England Coast Path will soon allow travellers to walk the entire English coast (Credit: Getty Images)</figcaption></figure><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Search for a larger territory like the United States and this disparity grows even further, from 12,380 miles (19,924km), according to the CIA World Factbook; to 84,000 miles (135,185km), according to the US Army Corps of Engineers; to an astonishing 95,471 miles (153,646km), according to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (Noaa), the federal agency responsible for mapping the US's coastline.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">One reason why these measurements vary so greatly, and why none is technically wrong, goes back to a curious discovery made by a British pacifist who was trying to understand war – and it's pitted nations against each other.</p><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="the-coastline-paradox" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="the-coastline-paradox" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">The coastline paradox</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In 1921, a Quaker mathematician and physicist named Lewis Fry Richardson wondered whether nations with longer shared borders were more likely to go to war. During his investigations, he noticed something peculiar: Spain reported its border with Portugal as 987km (613 miles), while Portugal measured it as 1,214km (754 miles). Belgium and the Netherlands also had a 69km (43 mile) disparity, and countries across the continent had similar disagreements.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Since these measurements were vitally important for a nation's sovereignty, Richardson wondered why the differences were so widespread. At the time, most countries measured their borders and coastlines by examining a map and placing rulers of equal length along their boundaries, so that one end of the ruler was always touching the other. But since almost no borders or coastlines are perfectly straight, Richardson realised that the smaller the ruler, the more curves it captured – and the longer the total measurement became.</p><div class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div class="sc-756ee424-1 hxpIws"><div data-testid="" class="sc-c2960217-0 hTubru"></div><button data-testid="player-play-button" aria-label="Play video" tabindex="0" class="sc-dec3f0fa-1 delfih"><div data-testid="cta-play-icon" class="sc-dec3f0fa-0 arsrU"><svg viewBox="0 0 82 82"><circle cx="41" cy="41" r="37" fill="#fff"></circle><polygon fill="#202224" points="32,25 32,58 60,42"></polygon></svg></div><div class="sc-dec3f0fa-4 bmCQlK"></div></button></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Many shoreline measurements appear to approach infinity</figcaption></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_2" id="dotcom-mid_2" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">This phenomenon became known as the <a target="_blank" href="https://www.britannica.com/science/coastline-paradox" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">coastline paradox</a>, and it applies to virtually all borders, boundaries and coastlines with especially jagged, twisting lines – like England's.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"Let me show you what this paradox looks like," said Danny Hyam, a senior geospatial consultant for the Ordnance Survey, Great Britain's national mapping agency.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"This is Britain's coastline at a scale of 1:1,000,000," he said, pulling up a broad, boxy border around the territory on his computer screen. "At that scale, it gives you a [coastline] measurement of 16,652km (10,347 miles). If I go down to 1:250,000, you start to see a bit more detail along the edges, islands appear that didn't before and that coastline is now 22,400km (13,919 miles). If we go to 1:50,000, jetties and more defined inlets appear, and our coast becomes 28,509km (17,715 miles) long. Then if I go to 1:2,500, the coastline becomes even more complicated, and it has now more than doubled in length."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Hyam added, "If I were to go down to 1:1, you'd be going around every barnacle, and suddenly our coastline would become longer, and longer and longer." As he explained, that is the paradox: the closer you look, the longer the coastline becomes.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllwz.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Getty Images If you were to measure every barnacle, grain of sand or atom along England's coastline, the measurement would approach infinity (Credit: Getty Images)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">If you were to measure every barnacle, grain of sand or atom along England's coastline, the measurement would approach infinity (Credit: Getty Images)</figcaption></figure></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_3" id="dotcom-mid_3" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In fact, Richardson found that were you to examine a coastline in ever-smaller units – down to every grain of sand or even every atom – the length of a coastline would appear to approach infinity. So, paradoxically, you could have a defined territory surrounded by a seemingly infinite boundary.</p><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="real-world-disputes" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="real-world-disputes" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">Real-world disputes</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Long before this paradox was fully understood, it was fuelling diplomatic tensions. In the late 19th Century, the British Empire and the US disagreed over where the border should divide British Columbia and Yukon from the Alaskan panhandle. One of the causes of this quarrel was the fact that each group was using a different sized ruler to measure the coastline, because an old treaty had declared, in highly vague terms, that it: "shall be formed by a line parallel to the winding of the coast".</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Alaska's complex, fjord-filled coastline winds a lot (so much so that according to Noaa, it's 9,000 miles (14,484km) longer than the equator). By using a larger ruler that cut across these fjords and inlets, Canada claimed better access to the Pacific Ocean. But by using a much smaller ruler that followed the complex "windings" of the shore, the US claimed control over the coast, and the dispute eventually had to be settled by an international tribunal.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Most recently in December 2024, India made headlines when it announced that its coastline had <a target="_blank" href="https://www.thehindu.com/sci-tech/science/the-maths-of-how-indias-coastline-lengthened-without-gaining-new-land/article69617103.ece" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">lengthened by more than 3,500km</a> (2,175 miles) without acquiring any new land – an increase of nearly 50%. One of the ways it did this was by essentially incorporating smaller rulers: it utilised higher-resolution software to capture the finer details of its many inlets, bays and offshore islands.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0ncllx5.jpg.webp 1376w, a border dispute between the British Empire and the US in the Alaska panhandle (Credit: Getty Images)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images)</figcaption></figure></div></div><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 bKjgsR"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-0 djNPjD"><div data-testid="dotcom-sid_2" id="dotcom-sid_2" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_4" id="dotcom-mid_4" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">As with the <a target="_blank" href="https://thecanadianencyclopedia.ca/en/article/alaska-boundary-dispute" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">Alaska Boundary Dispute</a> and the wildly different coastline lengths British, American and other national agencies report, all of these varying measurements are oddly valid.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"Because of the coastline paradox and how we define shoreline, none of these measurements is wrong; it's all dependent on the scale you're using," said Noaa cartographer Maryellen Sault, who said she regularly fields emails from confused people asking her why US coastline measurements vary so drastically, or how it's possible that <a target="_blank" href="https://www.pressherald.com/2025/12/08/is-maines-coastline-3-times-longer-than-californias-fact-brief/" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">Maine's shoreline is longer than California's</a>.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"><b id="more-like-this" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">More like this</b>:</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">• <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cy0dxexdd8xo" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">King opens world's longest coastal path around England</a></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">• <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/travel/article/20240505-an-epic-38-mile-hike-to-englands-northernmost-point" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">An epic 38-mile hike to England's northernmost point</a></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">• <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/travel/article/20250528-what-i-learned-from-walking-britains-entire-coast" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">What I learned from walking Britain's entire coast</a></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">All of this begs the question: why don't nations, states and organisations just come up with a standardised way to measure coastlines?</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"It's complicated," said Hyam. First, there's the question of tides. Since measuring a coastline at high tide versus low tide drastically changes its length, if you wanted to create a true standard, every coastline would need to be measured under identical tidal conditions at every point, which is virtually impossible.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllx7.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Getty Images Tides play a big role in a coastline's shape and measurement (Credit: Getty Images)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 jsSfKy">Tides play a big role in a coastline's shape and measurement (Credit: Getty Images)</figcaption></figure></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_5" id="dotcom-mid_5" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Then there's the issue of scale. Hyam explained that Britain maps its coastlines at a 1:10,000 scale because that's consistent with how land is also mapped across the territory, and because, "we've got a smaller country that's more [uniformly] dense". But in larger places like the US, Canada or Australia, there are vast swaths where "there's nothing there", so Hyam said that mapping every section at that scale would make far less practical sense.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Even if every country agreed to a standard scale, what would that be? A smaller unit of measurement may yield a more accurate coastline length. But if that measurement starts to approach an infinite number, are you actually getting closer to reality or further from it?</p><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="england's-shifting-coastline-and-path" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="england's-shifting-coastline-and-path" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">England's shifting coastline and path</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Scale and tides aside, the other challenge is that the Earth is constantly changing. Land erodes and accretes, and storms reshape coastlines. Depending on how exact you want to be, Hyam said, a coastline changes in some small way with every wave that breaks against it.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">England's new coastal path, which was conceived 18 years and seven prime ministers ago, was designed with these inevitable changes in mind. According to Neil Constable, who led the project for Natural England, the trail can be "rolled back" – moved inland – if the coastline erodes or shifts, "which is really important in a country where we've got a lot of eroding coast". This helps ensure that the trail remains usable for future generations.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0ncllyq.jpg.webp 1376w, Path will change as the English coastline changes (Credit: Getty Images)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Getty Images</span></div><figcaption class="sc-69115734-0 data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_6" id="dotcom-mid_6" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Recent months have shown how dynamic the new path can be. When heavy rains caused a <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/c1jkx0xy0rdo" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">landslide on part of the route in Dorset</a>, organisers moved the trail back 15m (49ft) and reopened it within weeks, adding an extra 1.5 miles (2.4km) to the trail's total length. As scientists warn that climate change is likely to bring heavier rains and rising seas, Constable knows that today's 2,689-mile (4,327km) path will surely change. "It'll be different in six months' time," he said. "Bits will have eroded, other parts will move inland, so it will keep changing with the coast."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Since the new English coastal path now links with the 870-mile (1,400km) <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-wales-61330672" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">Wales Coast Path</a>, it raises the possibility of a continuous coastal route ringing the entirety of Britain. "All that's missing is Scotland," Constable said. "But if you added in all of the Scottish coastline, because of its many winding lochs and the jagged nature of it, it would probably add many thousands of miles."</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Estimates for Scotland's coastline vary from 5,500 to 11,646 miles (8,850 to 18,742km), but of course, it's all in how you measure it.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">--</p><p mathematician and physicist named Lewis Fry Richardson wondered whether nations with longer shared borders were more likely to go to war. During his investigations, he noticed something peculiar: Spain reported its border with Portugal as 987km (613 miles), while Portugal measured it as 1,214km (754 miles). Belgium and the Netherlands also had a 69km (43 mile) disparity, and countries across the continent had similar disagreements.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"In 1921, a Quaker mathematician and physicist named Lewis Fry Richardson wondered whether nations with longer shared borders were more likely to go to war. During his investigations, he noticed something peculiar: Spain reported its border with Portugal as 987km (613 miles), while Portugal measured it as 1,214km (754 miles). Belgium and the Netherlands also had a 69km (43 mile) disparity, and countries across the continent had similar disagreements.","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Since these measurements were vitally important for a nation's sovereignty, Richardson wondered why the differences were so widespread. At the time, most countries measured their borders and coastlines by examining a map and placing rulers of equal length along their boundaries, so that one end of the ruler was always touching the other. But since almost no borders or coastlines are perfectly straight, Richardson realised that the smaller the ruler, the more curves it captured – and the longer the total measurement became.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Since these measurements were vitally important for a nation's sovereignty, Richardson wondered why the differences were so widespread. 16,652km (10,347 miles). If I go down to 1:250,000, you start to see a bit more detail along the edges, islands appear that didn't before and that coastline is now 22,400km (13,919 miles). If we go to 1:50,000, jetties and more defined inlets appear, and our coast becomes 28,509km (17,715 miles) long. In the late 19th Century, the British Empire and the US disagreed over where the border should divide British Columbia and Yukon from the Alaskan panhandle. gold_text: A new hiking trail will soon allow travellers to walk around England's entire coast – but a strange paradox means no one knows exactly how long it is. Last month, King Charles III inaugurated a new hiking path that will soon stretch around the entire coast of England. The project is roughly 80% complete, and when it fully opens later this year, the King Charles III England Coast Path will become the longest managed coastal walking path in the world. The 2,689-mile-long (4,327km) ramble connects the granite cliffs of Cornwall with the rolling sand dunes of Northumberland and East Sussex's iconic white chalk cliffs, allowing travellers to explore England's extensive shoreline step by step. But while the length of the newly designed path is easily measurable, the coastline that it follows is not. England's coast is often measured as part of the UK's, but look up how long that is and you'll get wildly different answers from various reputable organisations. The CIA World Factbook lists the UK's coastline as 7,723 miles (12,429km), while The World Resources Institute measures it at 12,251 miles (19,716km) – a discrepancy of more than 4,500 miles (7,242km). "The thing is, no one really knows exactly how long England's coastline is, or the United Kingdom's or most coastlines around the world, for that matter," said Victoria Braswell, a researcher and member of the Royal Geographical Society. "It's all in how you measure it." Search for a larger territory like the United States and this disparity grows even further, from 12,380 miles (19,924km), according to the CIA World Factbook; to 84,000 miles (135,185km), according to the US Army Corps of Engineers; to an astonishing 95,471 miles (153,646km), according to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (Noaa), the federal agency responsible for mapping the US's coastline. One reason why these measurements vary so greatly, and why none is technically wrong, goes back to a curious discovery made by a British pacifist who was trying to understand war – and it's pitted nations against each other. The coastline paradox In 1921, a Quaker mathematician and physicist named Lewis Fry Richardson wondered whether nations with longer shared borders were more likely to go to war. During his investigations, he noticed something peculiar: Spain reported its border with Portugal as 987km (613 miles), while Portugal measured it as 1,214km (754 miles). Belgium and the Netherlands also had a 69km (43 mile) disparity, and countries across the continent had similar disagreements. Since these measurements were vitally important for a nation's sovereignty, Richardson wondered why the differences were so widespread. At the time, most countries measured their borders and coastlines by examining a map and placing rulers of equal length along their boundaries, so that one end of the ruler was always touching the other. But since almost no borders or coastlines are perfectly straight, Richardson realised that the smaller the ruler, the more curves it captured – and the longer the total measurement became. This phenomenon became known as the coastline paradox, and it applies to virtually all borders, boundaries and coastlines with especially jagged, twisting lines – like England's. "Let me show you what this paradox looks like," said Danny Hyam, a senior geospatial consultant for the Ordnance Survey, Great Britain's national mapping agency. "This is Britain's coastline at a scale of 1:1,000,000," he said, pulling up a broad, boxy border around the territory on his computer screen. "At that scale, it gives you a [coastline] measurement of 16,652km (10,347 miles). If I go down to 1:250,000, you start to see a bit more detail along the edges, islands appear that didn't before and that coastline is now 22,400km (13,919 miles). If we go to 1:50,000, jetties and more defined inlets appear, and our coast becomes 28,509km (17,715 miles) long. Then if I go to 1:2,500, the coastline becomes even more complicated, and it has now more than doubled in length." Hyam added, "If I were to go down to 1:1, you'd be going around every barnacle, and suddenly our coastline would become longer, and longer and longer." As he explained, that is the paradox: the closer you look, the longer the coastline becomes. In fact, Richardson found that were you to examine a coastline in ever-smaller units – down to every grain of sand or even every atom – the length of a coastline would appear to approach infinity. So, paradoxically, you could have a defined territory surrounded by a seemingly infinite boundary. Real-world disputes Long before this paradox was fully understood, it was fuelling diplomatic tensions. In the late 19th Century, the British Empire and the US disagreed over where the border should divide British Columbia and Yukon from the Alaskan panhandle. One of the causes of this quarrel was the fact that each group was using a different sized ruler to measure the coastline, because an old treaty had declared, in highly vague terms, that it: "shall be formed by a line parallel to the winding of the coast". Alaska's complex, fjord-filled coastline winds a lot (so much so that according to Noaa, it's 9,000 miles (14,484km) longer than the equator). By using a larger ruler that cut across these fjords and inlets, Canada claimed better access to the Pacific Ocean. But by using a much smaller ruler that followed the complex "windings" of the shore, the US claimed control over the coast, and the dispute eventually had to be settled by an international tribunal. Most recently in December 2024, India made headlines when it announced that its coastline had lengthened by more than 3,500km (2,175 miles) without acquiring any new land – an increase of nearly 50%. One of the ways it did this was by essentially incorporating smaller rulers: it utilised higher-resolution software to capture the finer details of its many inlets, bays and offshore islands. As with the Alaska Boundary Dispute and the wildly different coastline lengths British, American and other national agencies report, all of these varying measurements are oddly valid. "Because of the coastline paradox and how we define shoreline, none of these measurements is wrong; it's all dependent on the scale you're using," said Noaa cartographer Maryellen Sault, who said she regularly fields emails from confused people asking her why US coastline measurements vary so drastically, or how it's possible that Maine's shoreline is longer than California's. More like this: • King opens world's longest coastal path around England • An epic 38-mile hike to England's northernmost point • What I learned from walking Britain's entire coast All of this begs the question: why don't nations, states and organisations just come up with a standardised way to measure coastlines? "It's complicated," said Hyam. First, there's the question of tides. Since measuring a coastline at high tide versus low tide drastically changes its length, if you wanted to create a true standard, every coastline would need to be measured under identical tidal conditions at every point, which is virtually impossible. Then there's the issue of scale. Hyam explained that Britain maps its coastlines at a 1:10,000 scale because that's consistent with how land is also mapped across the territory, and because, "we've got a smaller country that's more [uniformly] dense". But in larger places like the US, Canada or Australia, there are vast swaths where "there's nothing there", so Hyam said that mapping every section at that scale would make far less practical sense. Even if every country agreed to a standard scale, what would that be? A smaller unit of measurement may yield a more accurate coastline length. But if that measurement starts to approach an infinite number, are you actually getting closer to reality or further from it? England's shifting coastline and path Scale and tides aside, the other challenge is that the Earth is constantly changing. Land erodes and accretes, and storms reshape coastlines. Depending on how exact you want to be, Hyam said, a coastline changes in some small way with every wave that breaks against it. England's new coastal path, which was conceived 18 years and seven prime ministers ago, was designed with these inevitable changes in mind. According to Neil Constable, who led the project for Natural England, the trail can be "rolled back" – moved inland – if the coastline erodes or shifts, "which is really important in a country where we've got a lot of eroding coast". This helps ensure that the trail remains usable for future generations. Recent months have shown how dynamic the new path can be. When heavy rains caused a landslide on part of the route in Dorset, organisers moved the trail back 15m (49ft) and reopened it within weeks, adding an extra 1.5 miles (2.4km) to the trail's total length. As scientists warn that climate change is likely to bring heavier rains and rising seas, Constable knows that today's 2,689-mile (4,327km) path will surely change. "It'll be different in six months' time," he said. "Bits will have eroded, other parts will move inland, so it will keep changing with the coast." Since the new English coastal path now links with the 870-mile (1,400km) Wales Coast Path, it raises the possibility of a continuous coastal route ringing the entirety of Britain. "All that's missing is Scotland," Constable said. "But if you added in all of the Scottish coastline, because of its many winding lochs and the jagged nature of it, it would probably add many thousands of miles." Estimates for Scotland's coastline vary from 5,500 to 11,646 miles (8,850 to 18,742km), but of course, it's all in how you measure it.

class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"><b id="have-photographs-ever-really-told-the-truth?-one-hundred-and-fifty-years-before-today's-controversial-ai-chatbots-and-deep-fakes,-photographers-created-remarkable-image-manipulations.-here-are-10-images-from-the-19th-and-20th-centuries-that-tricked-the-viewer." class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">Have photographs ever really told the truth? One hundred and fifty years before today's controversial AI chatbots and deep fakes, photographers created remarkable image manipulations. Here are 10 images from the 19th and 20th Centuries that tricked the viewer.</b></p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Image manipulation and the unreliability of the visual media we consume is causing widespread concern. Elon Musk's controversial AI chatbot, Grok, which caused uproar after it was used to alter images of people so that they appear stripped of their clothing, is the latest in a series of sophisticated digital imaging tools that have falsified photographs and, in some cases, <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cvg1mzlryxeo" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">caused serious harm</a>. In January, following widespread criticism, this function was disabled for most users, and the European Commission <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/clye99wg0y8o" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">launched an investigation</a> into its use.</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">It will take more than this, however, to restore our faith in the photographic image. Since the early days of Photoshop in the 1990s, developments in image fakery have seen us looking at photographs with rising suspicion. But the Rijksmuseum's latest photography exhibition asks a pertinent question: Have photographs <i id="ever" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">ever</i> told the truth?</p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Focusing on images taken between 1860 and 1940, <a target="_blank" href="https://www.rijksmuseum.nl/en/whats-on/exhibitions/fake" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">Fake! Early Photo Collages and Photomontages from the Rijksmuseum Collection</a> makes the case that image fakery is far from a recent phenomenon and, when used wisely, can even be a force for good. From collages created with scissors and glue to clever deceptions fabricated under cover of darkness in their developing rooms, photographers have always enjoyed fooling their audiences. "Image manipulation has been around as long as photography itself," the exhibition’s curator, Hans Rooseboom, tells the BBC. "It’s part of the whole history of photography." Here are 10 images from the exhibition that tell the story of the early days of photographic trickery.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="1.-daydream-(c-1870–1890),-anonymous" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="1.-daydream-(c-1870–1890),-anonymous" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">1. Daydream (c 1870–1890), anonymous</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Two realities collide in this 19th-Century <i id="carte-de-visite" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">carte de visite</i> that was most likely purchased to be collected and traded. <i id="cartes-de-visite" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">Cartes de visite</i> were small mass-produced prints mounted on card, and were very popular in <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.com/culture/article/20220114-the-surprising-ways-that-victorians-flirted" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">the Victorian era</a>. In this one we see the present: a woman and her partner both with the tools of their trades; and an imagined future: her daydream of becoming a mother. The image, explains Rooseboom, was "a darkroom trick", achieved by shielding part of the photographic paper from the light and then adding a second negative to it later. Such images took photography into a new dimension, suggesting the innermost thoughts of their subjects, and paving the way for the comic strips of the future with their speech bubbles and thought clouds.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fp5j.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum (Credit: Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum</span></div></figure><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="2.-man-startled-by-his-own-reflection-(c-1870–1880),-leonard-de-koningh" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="2.-man-startled-by-his-own-reflection-(c-1870–1880),-leonard-de-koningh" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">2. Man startled by his own reflection (c 1870–1880), Leonard de Koningh</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">In this comical memento mori, where a man comes face to face with his ghost, the painter and photographer Leonard de Koningh exposed just half of the photographic plate, then had the subject adopt a different pose before exposing the other half. Photography might have been a relatively new art, but the transition between the two images is imperceptible. "It's like a magician," marvels Rooseboom. "You know you are being tricked, but you don't know how the photographer does it." Quoting Oscar Gustave Rejlander, a trailblazer for this type of composite printing, the photographer Robert Sobieszek (1943-2005) <a target="_blank" href="https://www.artforum.com/features/composite-imagery-and-the-origins-of-photomontage-part-i-the-naturalistic-strain-209228/" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">stated</a>: "This manner of working led not to falsehood but to truth. An image made by a single negative [claimed Rejlander] 'is not true, nor will it ever be so – the focus cannot be everywhere'."</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fp6k.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum (Credit: Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum</span></div></figure><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="3.-decapitation-(c-1880–1900),-fm-hotchkiss" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="3.-decapitation-(c-1880–1900),-fm-hotchkiss" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">3. Decapitation (c 1880–1900), FM Hotchkiss</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">"We still expect photography to bring the truth, but this idea only really emerged from the illustrated magazines of the 1930s in order to inform readers how things worked elsewhere in the world," says Rooseboom. Until then, the creative freedom to alter the image was unchallenged. "Anything possible would be tried out and produced," he says. "There was no ethical restraint on producing non-realistic images. No-one would forbid you from doing this." Removing and moving someone's head, for example, presented the photographer with a pleasing puzzle. In the case of this cabinet card – a style of print mounted on card had taken over from the smaller <i id="carte-de-visite" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">carte de visite</i> by the 1880s – with its black humour, the creative mission was highly successful. Only the positioning of the curtain, that would have concealed the original head, and some light retouching visible under a microscope, offer clues to how the photographer created the deception. </p></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div Rijksmuseum</span></div></figure><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="4.-photomontage-of-a-man-pushing-a-wheelbarrow-containing-a-head" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="4.-photomontage-of-a-man-pushing-a-wheelbarrow-containing-a-head" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">4. Photomontage of a man pushing a wheelbarrow containing a head</b></span><span id="" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"> </span><span id="(c-1900–1910),-anonymous" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="(c-1900–1910),-anonymous" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">(c 1900–1910), anonymous</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">This photomontage, created from two negatives, was found in a French photo album, and featured in the science magazine La Nature. Here, the displaced head illusion goes a step further as the photographer plays with scale, prefiguring <a target="_self" href="https://www.bbc.co.uk/culture/article/20241106-these-five-bizarre-surrealist-artworks-are-not-what-they-seem" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">the Surrealist movement</a> which gathered pace as the century unrolled, disrupting conventional shapes and sizes to create confusing, dreamlike scenes. The open doorway provides a conveniently plain, dark background in which to smuggle in the cut-and-pasted portion before re-photographing the image as a whole. Some of these images were most likely purchased as portraits to show others, to see their look of surprise. "It's hard to see where the trick starts and ends," says Rooseboom. "This is showing off. Something is unbelievable, impossible, it's improbable, but still it's there in a photographic image which suggests we see a real scene that really unfolded in front of the camera."</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" id="taking-our-geese-to-market-(1909),-martin-post-card-company" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">Taking our Geese to market (1909), Martin Post Card Company</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The trend for playing with images of impossible proportions spawned a genre known as "Exaggerations" or "Tall Tales". This US photograph was printed during the "Golden Age" of picture postcards, shortly after the US decreed that messages could be written on the address side of the card. We see again the pioneering role photo manipulation plays in artistic developments such as Surrealism, but the use of scale here is also a marketing ploy to create myths about the agricultural superiority of a region. In this case, it's the celebrated stuffed geese of Watertown, Wisconsin. Elsewhere in the show, Nebraska boasts about its bountiful produce with <a target="_blank" href="https://www.rijksmuseum.nl/assets/1b1b8af9-6156-484a-b2ef-522a32de0cde?w=640&h=640&fx=2048&fy=1267&format=webp&c=2cfd4d7022c7e59094d00a090c179a956c5f45223cfbe9633698a533919f2912" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN">an ear of corn</a> the length of a horse-drawn carriage. As US author and folklorist Roger Welsch noted in Tall Tale Postcards (1976): "Photography brought into being visual effects that tall-tale tellers through the centuries had seen only in their fertile imaginations."</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">6.</b></span><span id="" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"> </span><span id="car-floating-above-mulberry-bend-park,-new-york-(1908),-theodor-eismann" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="car-floating-above-mulberry-bend-park,-new-york-(1908),-theodor-eismann" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">Car floating above Mulberry Bend Park, New York (1908), Theodor Eismann</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Fake photographs activated the imagination, presenting imagined possibilities yet to come. This example of a <i id="toekomstbeeld" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">toekomstbeeld</i> (vision of the future) envisages a world where cars could fly. Elsewhere in the exhibition we see futuristic cityscapes: town centres transformed by sky rails and zeppelins thanks to some deft copy and pasting, and skyborne visitors floating over Boston, Hamburg and The Hague in scenes reminiscent of Mary Poppins. Eismann's New York photomontage [above] now features colour but is no more truthful, its limited range of inks added during the printing process at the whim of the designer.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fpb5.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum (Credit: Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum</span></div></figure><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="7.-advertisement-for-the-transfield-sisters-(c-1904-1918),-anonymous" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="7.-advertisement-for-the-transfield-sisters-(c-1904-1918),-anonymous" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">7. Advertisement for the Transfield Sisters (c 1904-1918), anonymous</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">As the 20th Century dawned, photography assumed a growing role in advertising, attracting attention with playful designs such as this early advert for the vaudeville act the Transfield Sisters. Photomontages used different-sized photographs, shot at a range of angles, which created, "a dynamic visual language, reflecting an era of rapid change". The marriage of advertising and photography brought about "an interplay between fiction and facts", explains Rooseboom. "This play between what you can believe and what you can't believe, what’s possible and impossible, that's the little game they are playing in all these images."</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 Rijksmuseum</span></div></figure><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="8." class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="8." class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">8.</b></span><span id="" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"> </span><span id="collision-between-a-car-and-a-steamroller-(1915),-alfred-stanley-johnson-jr" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="collision-between-a-car-and-a-steamroller-(1915),-alfred-stanley-johnson-jr" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">Collision between a car and a steamroller (1915), Alfred Stanley Johnson Jr</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">We've come to think of photo trickery as something sinister, but in his research on its use in early photography, Rooseboom says he was surprised to find that "three-quarters of all the images were made for fun". In this photomontage by Alfred Stanley Johnson Jr, the clever placement of a series of individual − sometimes overlapping − images provides a humorous snapshot in time. The unusually dynamic, action-filled scene features flying coattails and frilly bloomers as the passengers of a car are catapulted through the air, inviting a before-and-after narrative in the mind of the viewer. "Many photomontages obviously depict impossible situations," states a text at the exhibition. "The intention was not to mislead, but rather to entertain the viewer."</p></div></div></div></div><div data-component="advertisement-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 iLXgbm"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 jkCPav"><div data-testid="ad-unit" data-component="ad-slot" class="sc-88e34ec2-1 gPvGta"><div data-testid="dotcom-mid_4" id="dotcom-mid_4" class="dotcom-ad"></div></div></div></div></div><div data-component="layout-block" class="sc-9cd5bb24-0 lmzibM"><div class="sc-cd6075cf-0 DQtHs"><div class="sc-82b3c53b-0 gDQlgg"><div class="sc-85f9acf9-0 kfxpiP"><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img sizes="(min-width: 1280px) 50vw, (min-width: 1008px) 66vw, 96vw" srcset="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/160xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 160w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/240xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 240w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/320xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 320w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 480w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/640xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 640w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/800xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 800w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1024xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 1024w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1376xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 1376w, https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/1920xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp 1920w" src="https://ichef.bbci.co.uk/images/ic/480xn/p0n1fpcq.jpg.webp" loading="lazy" alt="Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum (Credit: Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum)" class="sc-5340b511-0 hLdNfA"><span class="sc-5340b511-2 jVqbAn">Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum</span></div></figure><h2 class="sc-64c6ac20-0 fWTqWp"><span id="9.-photo-collage-(1929),-albert-huyot" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="9.-photo-collage-(1929),-albert-huyot" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">9. Photo collage (1929), Albert Huyot</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">The cutting up of images and rearranging them on paper with glue was once a popular pastime. People made celebrity photo quizzes, sometimes featuring just a nose or a pair of eyes; and the photographed faces of friends and family were superimposed onto drawings for comic effect. Photo collages were also undertaken by established artists. In this piece, which is influenced by Dadaism and Cubism, French artist Albert Huyot manipulates fragments of photographic images into surprising new artistic forms. More intricate photographic artworks can be seen in the show on the pages of the Hungarian artist László Moholy-Nagy's seminal book Painting, Photography, Film (1925). In it he argues that photography is not just about recording reality. Instead, it should explore the visual language unique to its medium.</p><figure class="sc-70550624-0 bOanuU"><div data-testid="image" class="sc-5340b511-1 jJmNMc"><img src="https://static.files.bbci.co.uk/bbcdotcom/web/20260407-092955-f3cfe0ee04-web-3.0.0-2/grey-placeholder.png" class="sc-5340b511-0 gUePlo hide-when-no-script" aria-label="image unavailable"><img kORWLB"><b id="10." class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">10.</b></span><span id="" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"> </span><span id="mimicry-(joseph-goebbels-disguising-hitler-as-karl-marx-to-placate-the-workers)-(1934)," class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="mimicry-(joseph-goebbels-disguising-hitler-as-karl-marx-to-placate-the-workers)-(1934)," class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">Mimicry (Joseph Goebbels disguising Hitler as Karl Marx to placate the workers) (1934),</b></span><span id="" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"> </span><span id="john-heartfield" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kORWLB"><b id="john-heartfield" class="sc-4029fe79-0 kKtEQU">John Heartfield</b></span></h2><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV">Sometimes, in a surprising twist, photographic trickery was a tool for conveying a perceived truth. Anti-Nazi campaigner Helmut Herzfeld, who changed his name to the anglicised John Heartfield in protest against Hitler's regime, created over 200 handcrafted political photomontages for the leftist AIZ publication, many seeking to expose the hidden dangers of the Nazi dictatorship and the lies it disseminated. Mimicry depicts Joseph Goebbels, the Nazi Minister of Propaganda, disguising Hitler as the 19th-Century revolutionary communist Karl Marx. The artist is warning the working classes not to be fooled by Hitler's promises that he genuinely supports workers' rights. Such work is comparable with political memes today that aim to speak truth to power. Image manipulation can both mislead us and help us find our way. </p><p class="sc-1a18e57c-0 HooNV"><a target="_blank" href="https://www.rijksmuseum.nl/en/press/press-releases/fake-early-photo-collages-and-photomontages-from-the-rijksmuseum-collection#msdynmkt_trackingcontext=f168c204-7693-49fa-b659-66d194fb0200&msdynmkt_prefill=mktprf948c5887ec11476384fee8bc998885b2eoprf" class="sc-2554282c-0 cDYatN"><i id="fake!-early-photo-collages-and-photomontages-from-the-rijksmuseum-collection" class="sc-4029fe79-0 hTEgDj">Fake! Early Photo Collages and Photomontages from the Rijksmuseum Collection makes the case that image fakery is far from a recent phenomenon and, when used wisely, can even be a force for good. From collages created with scissors and glue to clever deceptions fabricated under cover of darkness in their developing rooms, photographers have always enjoyed fooling their audiences. \"Image manipulation has been around as long as photography itself,\" the exhibition’s curator, Hans Rooseboom, tells the BBC. \"It’s part of the whole history of photography.\" Here are 10 images from the exhibition that tell the story of the early days of photographic trickery.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Focusing on images taken between 1860 and 1940, ","attributes":[]}},{"type":"urlLink","model":{"text":"Fake! Daydream (c 1870–1890), anonymous","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"Two realities collide in this 19th-Century carte de visite that was most likely purchased to be collected and traded. Cartes de visite were small mass-produced prints mounted on card, and were very popular in the Victorian era. In this one we see the present: a woman and her partner both with the tools of their trades; and an imagined future: her daydream of becoming a mother. The image, explains Rooseboom, was \"a darkroom trick\", achieved by shielding part of the photographic paper from the light and then adding a second negative to it later. Such images took photography into a new dimension, suggesting the innermost thoughts of their subjects, and paving the way for the comic strips of the future with their speech bubbles and thought clouds.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Two realities collide in this 19th-Century ","attributes":[]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"carte de visite","attributes":["italic"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":" that was most likely purchased to be collected and traded. Man startled by his own reflection (c 1870–1880), Leonard de Koningh","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"2. Man startled by his own reflection (c 1870–1880), Leonard de Koningh","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"In this comical memento mori, where a man comes face to face with his ghost, the painter and photographer Leonard de Koningh exposed just half of the photographic plate, then had the subject adopt a different pose before exposing the other half. Photography might have been a relatively new art, but the transition between the two images is imperceptible. \"It's like a magician,\" marvels Rooseboom. \"You know you are being tricked, but you don't know how the photographer does it.\" Quoting Oscar Gustave Rejlander, a trailblazer for this type of composite printing, the photographer Robert Sobieszek (1943-2005) stated: \"This manner of working led not to falsehood but to truth. An image made by a single negative [claimed Rejlander] 'is not true, nor will it ever be so – the focus cannot be everywhere'.\"","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"In this comical memento mori, where a man comes face to face with his ghost, the painter and photographer Leonard de Koningh exposed just half of the photographic plate, then had the subject adopt a different pose before exposing the other half. Decapitation (c 1880–1900), FM Hotchkiss","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"\"We still expect photography to bring the truth, but this idea only really emerged from the illustrated magazines of the 1930s in order to inform readers how things worked elsewhere in the world,\" says Rooseboom. Until then, the creative freedom to alter the image was unchallenged. \"Anything possible would be tried out and produced,\" he says. \"There was no ethical restraint on producing non-realistic images. No-one would forbid you from doing this.\" Removing and moving someone's head, for example, presented the photographer with a pleasing puzzle. In the case of this cabinet card – a style of print mounted on card had taken over from the smaller carte de visite by the 1880s – with its black humour, the creative mission was highly successful. Only the positioning of the curtain, that would have concealed the original head, and some light retouching visible under a microscope, offer clues to how the photographer created the deception. ","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"\"We still expect photography to bring the truth, but this idea only really emerged from the illustrated magazines of the 1930s in order to inform readers how things worked elsewhere in the world,\" says Rooseboom. Photomontage of a man pushing a wheelbarrow containing a head","attributes":["bold","h1"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":" ","attributes":["h1"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"(c 1900–1910), anonymous","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"This photomontage, created from two negatives, was found in a French photo album, and featured in the science magazine La Nature. Here, the displaced head illusion goes a step further as the photographer plays with scale, prefiguring the Surrealist movement which gathered pace as the century unrolled, disrupting conventional shapes and sizes to create confusing, dreamlike scenes. The open doorway provides a conveniently plain, dark background in which to smuggle in the cut-and-pasted portion before re-photographing the image as a whole. Some of these images were most likely purchased as portraits to show others, to see their look of surprise. \"It's hard to see where the trick starts and ends,\" says Rooseboom. \"This is showing off. Something is unbelievable, impossible, it's improbable, but still it's there in a photographic image which suggests we see a real scene that really unfolded in front of the camera.\"","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"This photomontage, created from two negatives, was found in a French photo album, and featured in the science magazine La Nature. Taking our Geese to market (1909), Martin Post Card Company","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"5.","attributes":["bold","h1"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":" ","attributes":["h1"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Taking our Geese to market (1909), Martin Post Card Company","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"The trend for playing with images of impossible proportions spawned a genre known as \"Exaggerations\" or \"Tall Tales\". This US photograph was printed during the \"Golden Age\" of picture postcards, shortly after the US decreed that messages could be written on the address side of the card. We see again the pioneering role photo manipulation plays in artistic developments such as Surrealism, but the use of scale here is also a marketing ploy to create myths about the agricultural superiority of a region. In this case, it's the celebrated stuffed geese of Watertown, Wisconsin. Elsewhere in the show, Nebraska boasts about its bountiful produce with an ear of corn the length of a horse-drawn carriage. As US author and folklorist Roger Welsch noted in Tall Tale Postcards (1976): \"Photography brought into being visual effects that tall-tale tellers through the centuries had seen only in their fertile imaginations.\"","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"The trend for playing with images of impossible proportions spawned a genre known as \"Exaggerations\" or \"Tall Tales\". Elsewhere in the exhibition we see futuristic cityscapes: town centres transformed by sky rails and zeppelins thanks to some deft copy and pasting, and skyborne visitors floating over Boston, Hamburg and The Hague in scenes reminiscent of Mary Poppins. Advertisement for the Transfield Sisters (c 1904-1918), anonymous","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"As the 20th Century dawned, photography assumed a growing role in advertising, attracting attention with playful designs such as this early advert for the vaudeville act the Transfield Sisters. Photomontages used different-sized photographs, shot at a range of angles, which created, \"a dynamic visual language, reflecting an era of rapid change\". The marriage of advertising and photography brought about \"an interplay between fiction and facts\", explains Rooseboom. \"This play between what you can believe and what you can't believe, what’s possible and impossible, that's the little game they are playing in all these images.\"","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"As the 20th Century dawned, photography assumed a growing role in advertising, attracting attention with playful designs such as this early advert for the vaudeville act the Transfield Sisters. Collision between a car and a steamroller (1915), Alfred Stanley Johnson Jr","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"8.","attributes":["bold","h1"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":" ","attributes":["h1"]}},{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Collision between a car and a steamroller (1915), Alfred Stanley Johnson Jr","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"We've come to think of photo trickery as something sinister, but in his research on its use in early photography, Rooseboom says he was surprised to find that \"three-quarters of all the images were made for fun\". In this photomontage by Alfred Stanley Johnson Jr, the clever placement of a series of individual − sometimes overlapping − images provides a humorous snapshot in time. The unusually dynamic, action-filled scene features flying coattails and frilly bloomers as the passengers of a car are catapulted through the air, inviting a before-and-after narrative in the mind of the viewer. \"Many photomontages obviously depict impossible situations,\" states a text at the exhibition. \"The intention was not to mislead, but rather to entertain the viewer.\"","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"We've come to think of photo trickery as something sinister, but in his research on its use in early photography, Rooseboom says he was surprised to find that \"three-quarters of all the images were made for fun\". In this photomontage by Alfred Stanley Johnson Jr, the clever placement of a series of individual − sometimes overlapping − images provides a humorous snapshot in time. The unusually dynamic, action-filled scene features flying coattails and frilly bloomers as the passengers of a car are catapulted through the air, inviting a before-and-after narrative in the mind of the viewer. \"Many photomontages obviously depict impossible situations,\" states a text at the exhibition. \"The intention was not to mislead, but rather to entertain the viewer.\"","attributes":[]}}]}}]}},{"type":"advertisement","model":{"adType":"horizontal"},"incrementedType":"mid_4"},{"type":"image","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"altText","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"(Credit: Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum)","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"(Credit: Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum)","attributes":[]}}]}}]}}]}},{"type":"rawImage","model":{"height":1382,"width":1200,"locator":"/p0n1fpcq.jpg","originCode":"ic","copyrightHolder":"Courtesy of the Rijksmuseum"}}]}},{"type":"subheadline","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"9. Photo collage (1929), Albert Huyot","attributes":["bold","h1"]}}]}}]}}],"level":2}},{"type":"text","model":{"blocks":[{"type":"paragraph","model":{"text":"The cutting up of images and rearranging them on paper with glue was once a popular pastime. People made celebrity photo quizzes, sometimes featuring just a nose or a pair of eyes; and the photographed faces of friends and family were superimposed onto drawings for comic effect. Photo collages were also undertaken by established artists. In this piece, which is influenced by Dadaism and Cubism, French artist Albert Huyot manipulates fragments of photographic images into surprising new artistic forms. More intricate photographic artworks can be seen in the show on the pages of the Hungarian artist László Moholy-Nagy's seminal book Painting, Photography, Film (1925). In it he argues that photography is not just about recording reality. Instead, it should explore the visual language unique to its medium.","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"The cutting up of images and rearranging them on paper with glue was once a popular pastime. Anti-Nazi campaigner Helmut Herzfeld, who changed his name to the anglicised John Heartfield in protest against Hitler's regime, created over 200 handcrafted political photomontages for the leftist AIZ publication, many seeking to expose the hidden dangers of the Nazi dictatorship and the lies it disseminated. Mimicry depicts Joseph Goebbels, the Nazi Minister of Propaganda, disguising Hitler as the 19th-Century revolutionary communist Karl Marx. The artist is warning the working classes not to be fooled by Hitler's promises that he genuinely supports workers' rights. Such work is comparable with political memes today that aim to speak truth to power. Image manipulation can both mislead us and help us find our way. ","blocks":[{"type":"fragment","model":{"text":"Sometimes, in a surprising twist, photographic trickery was a tool for conveying a perceived truth.
